coredata.dc:title: Turn –on FRET-based cysteine sensor by sulfur-doped carbon dots and Au nanoparticles decorated WS2 nanosheet
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mirsadoughi, Ensiyeh
Nemati, Fatemeh
Oroojalian, Fatemeh
Hosseini, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: Cysteine is an essential biothiol that plays various functions in the human body. Decrease or exceeding of this excellent antioxidant from the expected range will lead to so many problems. Thus, appropriate sensing of it would be of great importance. Sulfur-doped carbon Dots(S-CDs) owe excellent fluorescence emission. Therefore, designing a Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between S-CDs as donor and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) decorated tungsten disulfide nanosheet (WS2 NSs) would be a perfect strategy for cysteine detection. Excitation at 340 nm will give the maximum quantum yield of S-CDs (21%) and fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm. In the presence of cysteine, the FRET mechanism inhibited through the affinity of cysteine's functional groups (-SH and -NH2) toward AuNPs and S-CDs fluorescence emission was recovered. To find the best efficiency of the system, optimization of pH, temperature, and time was investigated. Here the linear range of 3–275 µM and limit of detection of 0.01 µM was obtained. Finally, the fluorescence method was applied to the analysis of cysteine in human blood serum, which poses the potential of rapid and sensitive sensing. It can detect both lower and higher amounts of serum cysteine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
S-CDs
WS2 nanosheet
Au nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The determination of cysteine in real samples
Characterization of S-CDs and Au-NPs decorated WS2 NSs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of University of Tehran
North Khorasan University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 98016067), the North Khorasan University of Medical Sciences (NKUMS 990206) and the Research Council of University of Tehran for the financial supports of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) EDS spectra, (b) TEM image, (c) FTIR spectrum, and (d) fluorescence emission spectra of S-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 410 nm.
(a) EDS spectra, (b) TEM image, (c) FTIR analysis, and (d) XRD patterns of Au-decorated WS2 NSs.

Fluorescence emission of S-CDs (blue) in the presence of Au-decorated WS2 NSs (red) was quenched and then recovered by cysteine (green). Inset: photographs of S-CDs, S-CDs plus Au- decorated WS2 NSs, and S-CDs plus Au-decorated WS2 NSs in the presence of cysteine under the UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of S-CDs in the attendance of different concentration of Au-decorated WS2 NSs. (b) The correlation of (F0/F) (where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs without and with Au-decorated WS2 NSs, respectively) on the concentration of Au-decorated WS2 NSs.
(a) Represents the fluorescence emission recovery of S-CDs in different cysteine amounts. (b) Is the dependence of (F-F0)/F0 to concentration of cysteine.

Schematic illustration of the FRET mechanism between S-CDs and Au-decorated WS2 NSs, the fluorescence emission of S-CDs was quenched by Au-decorated WS2 NSs, and then was restored in the presence of cysteine.
Comparison of present work with other methods for cysteine detection.
Analytical results for cysteine monitoring in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Turn –on FRET-based cysteine sensor by sulfur-doped carbon dots and Au nanoparticles decorated WS2 nanosheet

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Advanced Technologies , School of Medicine, North Khorasan University of Medical Sciences, Bojnurd, Iran (F. Oroojalian). sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Sciences and Technologies in Medicine
North Khorasan University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cysteine is an essential biothiol that plays various functions in the human body. Decrease or exceeding of this excellent antioxidant from the expected range will lead to so many problems. Thus, appropriate sensing of it would be of great importance. Sulfur-doped carbon Dots(S-CDs) owe excellent fluorescence emission. Therefore, designing a Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between S-CDs as donor and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) decorated tungsten disulfide nanosheet (WS2 NSs) would be a perfect strategy for cysteine detection. Excitation at 340 nm will give the maximum quantum yield of S-CDs (21%) and fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm. In the presence of cysteine, the FRET mechanism inhibited through the affinity of cysteine's functional groups (-SH and -NH2) toward AuNPs and S-CDs fluorescence emission was recovered. To find the best efficiency of the system, optimization of pH, temperature, and time was investigated. Here the linear range of 3–275 µM and limit of detection of 0.01 µM was obtained. Finally, the fluorescence method was applied to the analysis of cysteine in human blood serum, which poses the potential of rapid and sensitive sensing. It can detect both lower and higher amounts of serum cysteine.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine is a non-essential amino acid and significant biothiol that participates in essential functional processes in the human body like protein synthesis, metabolism, cell detoxification, and a tremendous antioxidant molecule [1]. As the antioxidant, it prohibits oxidative damages of cystine/cysteine redox cycle [2]. Besides, it is a Biomarker of cancer, HIV, sepsis [3]. Cysteine normal range in the mammalian cell is (30–200) μM [4], and its high or low level will cause severe problems. As reported, the increment of it generates neurotoxicity and cardiovascular disease, while its deficiency leads to skin damage, cancer, reduced hematopoiesis [1], growth retardation, and liver damage [5]. Owing to biological matter of cysteine, establishing a suitable approach for sensitive and selective sensing is of great interest, and drastic measures have been done for it. Methods like High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], colorimetric [7], electrochemistry [8], and fluorometric [9] have been reported for cysteine determination. In comparison with the colorimetric methods, fluorescence ones have a higher sensitivity, characteristic emission spectrum in case of particular excitation wavelength [10], the merits of short response time, and simplicity[11]. It is noteworthy to mention that fluorescence probes with dyes and radioactive labels pose difficulties concerning the photobleaching effect in dyes, causing short emission time and the problems related to the disposal and handling of radioactive probes [12]. FRET is a technique for surveying interactions between two close molecules, states as donor and acceptor. This process leads to transferring emission energy from donor to acceptor [13] in case of the proximity of less than 10 nm [14]. As a result of FRET, photoluminescence quenching or activation occurs [15]. For example, Y. Niu et al. constructed a FRET assembly using gold nanocluster stabilized with bovine serum albumin for cysteine detection [16]. Yuye Chen et al. designed a switching fluorescence sensor-based CDs and AuNPs for the determination of cysteine [17]. Jian-Jun Li et al. developed a turn-on Fluorescence system for L-cysteine detection based on FRET between Au-Ag nanoclusters and Au nanorods [18]. CDs, as a FRET donor with a size less than 10 nm [19], are categorized in zero-dimensional nanomaterials in the carbon family [20]. They benefit from special optical performance, suitable biocompatibility, simple surface fabrication, and low toxicity [21]. These nanoparticles also pose photostability, high chemical features, and simple preparation procedures [20]. Doping and surface functionalization of CDs have aroused significant attention to enhance their fluorescence properties [22]. WS2 NSs, as a member of two-dimensional transition metal chalcogenides (TMDs), can be a great candidate for FRET acceptor and quencher. Its 3-layered structure caused by S-W-S through van der Waals interactions has magnetic properties like large surface area, narrowband gaps [23], and broad absorption spectrum [19]. This graphene-like structure [24] has an interlayer spacing way larger than commercial graphite (0.63 nm compared to that of graphite 0.34). The number of active sites on the WS2 NSs has decreased due to strong inter-sheet π-π interaction and layer stacking [25]. Therefore, by doping or decorating nanoparticles or Nobel metals, the properties of the WS2 NSs will be improved. In a gas sensor, it has been reported that decorating Nobel metals with catalytic effects on the surface of the WS2 NSs will decline energy for the interaction of gas molecules. Besides, decorating WS2 NSs by AuNPs will gain better sensing properties of nanosheets [26]. The present work aims to design a novel turn-on fluorescence sensor for fast, sensitive, and accurate cysteine detection in human blood serum based on FRET mechanism between sulfur-doped carbon quantum dot and AuNPs decorated on tungsten disulfide nanosheet. For this, we have made S-CDs through a one-pot hydrothermal method by having citric acid and sodium sulfide as the source of carbon and sulfur, consequently. The synthesis of the nanosheet has three steps. First, WO3 nanosheet by hydrothermal method, WS2 NSs in furnace and AuNPs decorated through sonication. In the presence of WS2 NSs fluorescence emission of S-CDs was quenched via FRET, and cysteine caused the formation of Au-S and Au-N bonds on the surface of Au-NPs decorated WS2 NSs. As stopping FRET, the fluorescence emission was recovered. Scheme 1 .

The citric acid (+99%), thiourea, glutathione, cysteine, and other amino acids, were purchased from Merck. Sodium sulfide, sodium tungstate, nitric acid, HAuCL4, all used chemicals, were of analytical grade. Deionized water with a resistivity of more than 18 MΩ, (acquired from a Millipore Milli-Q system) was used for the preparation of the solution.

The fluorescence measurements were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were carried out in the range of 200–800 nm using Perkin-Elmer lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (400–4000 cm−1) were recorded on a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer in KBr pellets at room temperature. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was obtained using a Tescan model energy dispersive spectrometer. The morphologies of samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (Zeiss, EM10C, 80 KV, Germany). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were obtained on a Philips diffractometer (model X'Pert MPD). Zeta potential was achieved by the zetasizer (Marvalen, Model of Nano-ZS). The pH adjustments and measurements were performed by pH meter Metrohm.

S-CDs were fabricated through a hydrothermal process. According to the literature, 0.5 gr of citric acid was dispersed in 50 ml ultra-pure water and stirred. By adding 0.142 mg Na2S to the as-prepared citric acid solution and mixing for 10 min, the whole mixture was transferred to autoclave and heated in oven at 180 °C for 12 h [27]. The obtained material was dissolved in 40 ml of deionized water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min for removing large particles. Consequently, the resultant supernatant was filtered with a cylinder membrane filter (0.22 μm), and the obtained suspension containing S-CDs was collected for further investigation and use.

WO3 NSs synthesized through slight changes from the former reported method [28]. Naturally, 400 mg of Na2WO4·2H2O was dissolved in a 300 ml nitric acid solution (4.8 mol L−1). After stirring well for 24 h at 25° C, the mixture was transferred to autoclave and heated at 180° C for 3 h.

For the fabrication of the WS2 NSs, the following measures were done, 1 mmol WO3 NSs and 60 mmol thiourea milled in a mortar for 30 min. Then, the blended was transferred to the crucible, and heated in a furnace at 850°C for 1 h at a rate of 10° C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere [29].

Au NPs decorated on the surface of WS2 NSs, by the following procedure. 0.4 g of HAuCl4 was dispersed in 2-propanol. Then, WS2 NSs were added to the mentioned solution and heated for 30 min, and finally, the sample was sonicated for 45 min [23].

For cysteine detection, S-CDs solution was diluted, and the concentration of (0.6 mg/ml) was added to 1 ml PBS buffer at pH 5 at room temperature. After adding 1 ml Au-decorated WS2 NSs 16.66 µg/ml, the mixed solution was transferred to a 3 ml fluorescence cuvette. A different amount of cysteine standard solution was added to the cuvette. After incubation of 7 min, fluorescence spectra were measured with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the slit widths of 10 for both excitation and emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of cysteine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For monitoring system practicality toward real samples, the human serum of healthy candidates was gained from Masoud laboratory in Tehran, Iran. The samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm, and was diluted 20 times. Based on the standard addition method, the various cysteine concentrations (5, 10, and 15 µM) were added to the two different serum samples according to 2.7. section.

The quantum yield of as-prepared S-CDs was calculated to be 21% by having quinine sulfate as the standard fluorescence source, based on the (Eq. (1)):
(1)
Yu =
Y st
I u
/
I st
A st
/
A u
n
u
2
/
n
st
2

Where I is the total fluorescence emission intensity, A stands out as the optical density, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and Y is the quantum yield. The subscript is “u” backing to sample and “st” for the standard with known QY [30,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S-CDs and Au-NPs decorated WS2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: Transition electron microscopy (Fig. 1 b), confirmed that S-CDs were uniformly dispersed spherical quantum dots with a qualified distribution of particle size in the range of 3–9 nm. The IR spectrum exhibited a functional group of S-CDs (Fig. 1c). The strong band at 3437 cm−1 and medium intensity band at 2928 cm−1 were owing to stretching vibration of (O-H) and (C-H), respectively. The absorption band located at 2330 cm−1 confirmed the presence of S-H, while a sharp peak at 1716 cm−1 and bending at 1020 was associated with stretching vibration (C=O). The absorption at 1640 cm−1 corresponded to the C=C stretching modes. The peak at 673 cm−1 was ascribed to C-S-/C-S-C bending. The attendance of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, will pave the road for further hydrophilicity and stability of CDs [27,31,32]. By these results, modification of sulfur on the surface of CDs was confirmed. Investigation of the components S-CDs was carried out using EDX. The attendance of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur was validated as demonstrated in the EDX spectrum in Fig. 1a. The excitation-dependent behavior is an excellent indicator of CDs. By the excitation wavelength from 340 to 410 nm (Fig. 1d), an apparent redshift and decline in the maximum peak were observed, and the highest quantum yield of S-CDs (21%) with a maximum height at 460 nm was earned while exciting at 340 nm. The heterogeneous size of CDs would be the primary justification of this. Also, some physical theories may explain this excitation dependence characteristic, like quantum confinement, heteroatom model, surface edge model, and surface trap model. However, the actual process is still ambiguous [32]. Hence, the excitation wavelength of 340 nm was chosen as the optimum wavelength, and used for the following experiments. Fig. 2 a presented the elemental composition of the Au-decorated WS2 NSs carried out by EDX, and the attendance of elements W, S, Au, N was confirmed. Fig. 2b prepared the TEM images of Au-decorated WS2 NSs, which proved the layer-structure of WS2 NSs, and AuNPs successfully located on the surface of WS2 NSs. Fig. 2c showed the FTIR spectrum of the Au-decorated WS2 NSs where peaks at 758 cm−1, 931 cm−1and 2430 cm−1 are depicted as the functional groups of W–S, S–S, and S-H. The observed intense peak at 1384 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 band ascribed to stretchingdeformationof the hydroxyl group while the band at 3437 cm−1 was assigned to OH vibration [33,34]. The XRD graph of Au-decorated WS2 NSs is illustrated in Fig. 2d. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 29.08°, 31.67°, 33.32°, 38.97°, 45.57°, 48.27°, 60.72° were corresponding to (004), (100), (101), (103), (006), (105), and (112) planes of WS2. Also, 2θ of 23.07°, 28.01°, 36.60°, 67.47°, attributing to diffractions of (100), (200), (201), and (222) of WO3 planes, emphasized that Au-decorated WS2 NSs encompassed WO3 [24,35].

The process of fluorescence quenching could be in static or dynamic form [31]. Besides, the ratio of fluorescence reducing emission would be classified as photo-induced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), and FRET. FRET is a non-radiative form of transferring energy from the excited donor to the acceptor molecule. For the occurrence of FRET, having a spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor is required [13]. Besides, an appropriate direction of the two dipole moments is necessitated, and another vital factor that distinguishes FRET from IFE is a short distance between donor and acceptor. In FRET systems, the diameter between donor and acceptor is less than 10 nm, while in IFE, there is no need for small space or any unique covalent linkage [14,36]. It is shown in Fig. 3 that by excitation at 340 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity of S-CDs earned at 460 nm, and in the presence of 16.66 µg/ml Au-decorated WS2 NSs quenching happened. Having cysteine in the system, the fluorescence emission was recovered due to the high affinity of the functional groups of cysteine (-SH and -NH2) to AuNPs [37]. Thus, S-CDs were separated from Au-decorated WS2 NSs, and the FRET mechanism stopped. Quenching and recovering in the presence of Au-decorated WS2 NSs and 275 µM of cysteine is illustrated in Fig. 3, as well as the picture of cuvette under UV light in which the blue photoluminescence of S-CDs is obvious. Spectral overlap of more than 30% between fluorescence emission spectra of donor and UV absorption of accepter is necessary for FRET occurrence [14]. As displayed in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission of S-CDs as the donor had a maximum peak at 460 nm while exciting at 340 nm, and it had appropriate overlap with UV absorption of nanosheet. Au-decorated WS2 NSs had two adsorption peaks. The peaks at 340 and 550 nm corresponded to UV absorption of WS2 NSs and AuNPs, respectively. These results are firmly in favor of FRET happening. For validating the occurrence of FRET in the presence of a quencher, several studies have been carried out. By increasing of Au-decorated WS2 NSs from 1.6 to 16.66 µg/ml, (Fig. 4 a) the fluorescence emission of S-CDs was quenched, and the maximum FRET efficiency (E) of 63 % obtained by the concentration of 16.66 µg/ml of Au-decorated WS2 NSs as calculated according to the formula (Eq.2) [14]:
(2)
E = 1 -
I DA
/
I D

Where IDA and ID were the fluorescence intensity of donor in the presence and absence of acceptor, respectively. This result revealed that fluorescence resonance energy had transferred effectively from S-CDs to Au-decorated WS2 NSs. The Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (3)) was used in calibration curves of various amounts of Au-decorated WS2 NSs added CDs to investigate the quenching mechanism. (Fig. 4b)
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
C

Where KSV and C represented the quenching constant and concentration of the quencher, respectively. F0 and F were the fluorescence emission in the absence and presence of acceptor. By plotting the concentration of Au-decorated WS2 NSs versus the quenching rate of fluorescence intensity, F0/F = 0.0981x + 1.0674 was obtained, and the quenching constant was 0.0981. As both static and dynamic reducing of fluorescence intensity would be matched in the Stern-Volmer equation, having time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the components in the system is of great importance [31]. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of S-CDs without and with the acceptor were studied and are demonstrated in Fig. S2. S-CDs were conformed to the biexponential model, and they owned two fluorescence lifetimes. The short and long lifetimes of S-CDs were supposed to be related to carbon center and surface trap in order [38]. The average fluorescence lifetime of S-CDs (τD) was gained to be 5.3 ns by the sum of the ∑biτi products. In contrast, in the presence of 16.66 µg/ml Au-decorated WS2 NSs, the diminishing in fluorescence lifetime was observed (τDA = 2 ns). This decrement of fluorescence lifetime was in favor of dynamic quenching and FRET affirmation. Here FRET transfer efficiency was 63% based on the equation (Eq. (4)), [38].
(4)
η FRET = 1 -
τ DA
/
τ D

Where (τD)and (τDA) were the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and attendance of quencher, respectively. For further explanation of FRET occurrence, Zeta potential was studied, and the results are in Fig. S3. The positively charged S-CDs (+5) and negatively charge Au-decorated WS2 NSs (−16) will have an appropriate electrostatic interaction, leading to truncating the space between S-CDs and Au-decorated WS2 NSs. Thus, the possibility of IFE was excluded.

The effect of pH, temperature, and time were studied to find the best FRET efficiency. Cysteine has free thiol, amine, and carboxyl groups which can be influenced by acidic and alkaline pH. Thus, the impact of pH was investigated by PBS buffer. As it is shown in Fig. S4(a) by increasing pH from 2 to 11, the optimum FRET efficiency gained at pH 5, while in alkaline range due to deprotonation of functional groups of cysteine, it had fewer attractions to AuNPs of the Au-decorated WS2 NSs and efficiency of the system decreased [9]. The quantum yield efficiency of the exciting photon relies on both radiative and non-radiative decay rates. Since FRET is a non-radiative form of releasing energy, the decay rate is temperature-dependent, and the higher temperature we have, the less quantum yield will be obtained. Boltzmann distribution as A ⋅ − exp (-E / kT) can reasonably justify the non-radiative decay rate of this temperature correlation where A is a constant, E is ground state energy, k is the Boltzmann's constant, and T is the thermodynamic temperature. As it is depicted in Fig. S4(b), reducing of fluorescence emission ratio was obtained by increasing temperature from 25∘ C to 45∘ C [11,31]. Fig. S4(c) indicates the time impact on the recovery of the fluorescence emission in the system. After 7 min of incubation with cysteine in the presence of S-CDs and Au-decorated WS2 NSs, the maximum emission rate reached, and from 7 to 30 min, F-F0/F0 had equilibrium. As a result, pH = 5, the temperature of 25∘ C, and the 7 min of incubation were selected to be the optimum agents for further analysis.

By introducing cysteine to the quenched sample, the fluorescence emission was restored (Fig. 5 a). The possible explanation might be due to the reaction of AuNPs toward thiol and amine groups of cysteine, which leads to dissociation of S-CDs from Au-decorated WS2 NSs and enhancing fluorescence emission. As it is depicted in Fig. 5b, the variation of fluorescence intensity of S-CDs showed a linear range of cysteine concentration from 3 to 275 μM and a detection limit of 0.01 μM. Table 1 demonstrated that the present nanosensor has the merit of the wide range of cysteine detection, including the detection of lower and higher levels of cysteine in blood serum.

Seven biomolecules were tested in the presence of S-CDs and Au-decorated WS2 NSs. For studying any cysteine determination interference, 100 µM of each amino acid (methionine, tryptophan, arginine, phenylalanine, glycine, and glutamic acid) in pH 5 were added to the dispersion separately. As none of these amino acids had a free -SH group, no interference for cysteine detection was observed. Cysteine attaches to AuNPs by its -SH and -NH2 groups. Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide composed of glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine. γ-carboxyl group (-COOH) of glutamic acid and amino (-NH2) group of cysteine make a peptide bond. Therefore, linkage of GSH toward AuNPs is only through the -SH bond [37], and 100 µM of GSH had no interference on cysteine determination. Also, the selectivity of the sensor conducted in the presence of various ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+), and the ratio of F-F0/F0 was almost below 0.1 for all ions tested. As a result, our sensor has specific response to cysteine (Fig. S4(d)).

For monitoring the practicality of the designed system, two different human serums (1 and 2) were applied, and the concentration of 5, 10, and 15 µM of cysteine were spiked. In Table 2 , the recovery of 95–104% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) <3.7% (n = 3) was obtained. These results showed that the proposed platform could be used to sense cysteine in human serum with the advantage of high simplicity and sensitivity.

In summary, S-CDs fluorescence emission was quenched by Au-decorated WS2 NSs based on Au-S linkage on the surface of WS2 NSs. S-CDs were replaced by cysteine due to the high affinity of functional groups of cysteine toward AuNPs, and the fluorescence emission was recovered. The designed turn-on FRET system was applied for cysteine determination in human blood serum. The selectivity of this platform was carried out in the presence of different aminoacids and ions, and no interference was observed. Thus, the prepared nanosensor had a high degree of accuracy, selectivity, low cost, and fast response.

Ensiyeh Mirsadoughi and Fatemeh Nemati designed and performed the experiments and also wrote the manuscript. Fatemeh Oroojalian, Morteza Hosseini as the corresponding author designed experiments, sponsored the study and made all the arrangements. All the authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 98016067), the North Khorasan University of Medical Sciences (NKUMS 990206) and the Research Council of University of Tehran for the financial supports of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120903.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of cysteine based on a coumarin–rhodol derivative
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Bai, M.-X. Wu, Q.-J. Ma, C.-Y. Wang, J.-G. Sun, M.-J. Tian, J.-S. Li, A FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of cysteine based on a coumarin–rhodol derivative, New Journal of Chemistry. 43 (2019) 14763–14771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel NIR fluorescence probe with cysteine-activated structure for specific detection of cysteine and its application in vitro and in vivo
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ge, F. Shen, Y. Yin, K. Chang, X. Zhang, P. Zhou, J. Li, Y. Liu, C. Lu, A novel NIR fluorescence probe with cysteine-activated structure for specific detection of cysteine and its application in vitro and in vivo, Talanta. 223 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121758.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel fluorometric assay for detection of cysteine as a reducing agent and template in formation of copper nanoclusters
J. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.S. Borghei, M. Hosseini, M. Khoobi, M.R. Ganjali, Novel Fluorometric Assay for Detection of Cysteine as a Reducing Agent and Template in Formation of Copper Nanoclusters, Journal of Fluorescence. 27 (2017) 529–536. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10895-016-1980-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive fluorescent probe for imaging biothiol in zebrafish
Dyes Pigm.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and synthesis of a novel “turn-on” long range measuring fluorescent probe for monitoring endogenous cysteine in living cells and Caenorhabditis elegans
Anal. Chim. Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of total homocysteine, cysteine, glutathione, and N-acetylcysteine in brain homogenates by HPLC
